questions.question: Explain the difference between internal fragmentation and external fragmentation. Which memory management techniques suffer from each type?
keyPoints: Internal fragmentation: wasted space within allocated memory block due to fixed-sized allocation units
External fragmentation: free memory scattered in small non-contiguous blocks
Paging suffers from internal fragmentation (unused space within pages/frames)
Contiguous allocation and segmentation suffer from external fragmentation

questions.question: Describe the steps involved in handling a page fault in a demand paging system.
keyPoints: 1. Hardware generates trap to OS
2. OS saves process state and checks page table
3. If page is valid but not in memory, determine where on disk
4. Find a free frame in physical memory
5. Issue disk I/O to read the required page into the frame
6. Update page table entry (valid bit, frame number)
7. Restore process state and restart the interrupted instruction

questions.question: What is thrashing? Explain the working set model as a solution to thrashing.
keyPoints: Thrashing: excessive paging activity causing CPU to spend more time on page I/O than execution
Caused when processes don't have enough frames for their working set
Working set: pages referenced in recent time interval τ
Solution: OS ensures each process has enough frames to hold its working set
This minimizes page faults and improves CPU utilization

questions.question: Compare FIFO and LRU page replacement algorithms. Why is LRU generally preferred over FIFO?
keyPoints: FIFO evicts oldest page in memory regardless of usage
LRU evicts least recently used page based on temporal locality
FIFO suffers from Belady's Anomaly (increasing frames can increase faults)
LRU better approximates Optimal algorithm
LRU tracks page usage history making better replacement decisions

questions.question: Explain the concept of virtual memory and its advantages.
keyPoints: Virtual memory creates illusion of larger memory than physically available
Uses demand paging to load pages only when needed
Provides memory protection between processes
Enables processes larger than physical memory to execute
Simplifies programming by removing need for memory management

questions.question: What is copy-on-write (COW)? How does it optimize memory usage during fork()?
keyPoints: COW allows parent and child to share same memory pages after fork()
Initially, pages are marked read-only and shared
When either process modifies a page, a private copy is made
Reduces memory overhead when fork() is followed by exec()
Improves performance by deferring or avoiding page copying

questions.question: Explain the structure of an inverted page table. What are its advantages over a traditional page table?
keyPoints: Inverted page table has one entry per physical frame
Each entry stores: physical frame number, virtual page number, process ID
Advantages: reduces memory overhead for large address spaces
Disadvantages: slower translation, difficulty implementing sharing
Used in systems with large address spaces like IBM mainframes

questions.question: Explain the following page replacement algorithms with an example: FIFO, LRU, and Optimal. Also discuss Belady's Anomaly.
keyPoints: FIFO: Evicts oldest page; simple but suffers from Belady's Anomaly
LRU: Evicts least recently used; better but requires hardware/software support
Optimal: Evicts page that will not be used longest in future; theoretical best
Belady's Anomaly: In FIFO, increasing frames can sometimes increase page faults
Example showing anomaly: with reference string 1,2,3,4,1,2,5,1,2,3,4,5 - 3 frames = 9 faults, 4 frames = 10 faults

questions.question: A computer system has 16KB of physical memory with page size of 1KB. The logical address space of a process is 32KB. Answer the following: (i) How many bits are in a logical address? (ii) How many bits are in a physical address? (iii) How many pages per process? (iv) How many frames in physical memory? (v) How many entries in the page table assuming each entry is 2 bytes?
keyPoints: (i) Logical: 32KB = 2^15 bytes, so 15 bits
(ii) Physical: 16KB = 2^14 bytes, so 14 bits
(iii) Pages = 32KB/1KB = 32 pages = 2^5 (5 bits for page number)
(iv) Frames = 16KB/1KB = 16 frames = 2^4 (4 bits for frame number)
(v) Page table entries = 32 (one per page), size = 32 × 2 = 64 bytes

questions.question: Describe the various frame allocation algorithms in memory management. Compare equal allocation, proportional allocation, and priority-based allocation.
keyPoints: Equal allocation: Each process gets same number of frames regardless of size
Proportional allocation: Frames allocated proportional to process size or priority
Priority allocation: Higher priority processes get more frames
Each has trade-offs: fairness vs efficiency vs responsiveness
Global vs local replacement affects allocation strategies

questions.question: Explain segmentation with paging. How does it combine the advantages of both techniques? Draw the address translation mechanism.
keyPoints: Logical address: segment-number + offset
Segment table maps segment number to segment descriptor (base, limit, etc.)
Offset is checked against limit, then added to base to get linear address
Linear address divided into: page directory index, page table index, offset
Page table maps to physical frame number
Combines logical view of segmentation with efficiency of paging
Provides protection at segment level and eliminates external fragmentation
